Psalm 111 invites you into a peculiar kind of worship—one built not on feeling but on examination. The psalmist does not sing because emotion swept him away; he sings because he has *pondered* the works of the Lord, because he has looked closely and found them worthy of praise. This matters more than we usually admit. Your worship is meant to be intelligent, rooted in what you actually know about who God is and what He has done. The psalmist holds up God's works to the light like a jeweler studying a stone, and what emerges is a portrait of a God who is simultaneously majestic and attentive—His work splendid and grand, yet His hand extended to feed those who fear Him. He does not stand distant from the covenant He made; He remembers it. This is the God who meets you: one whose righteousness endures forever, whose justice and truth form the very structure of His dealings with His people.

What makes this psalm radical is its refusal of sentimentality. The psalmist does not ask you to feel grateful; he calls you to *understand* the connection between God's character and His actions. He has shown His people the power of His works by giving them an inheritance—power demonstrated not in spectacular isolation but in provision, protection, and the establishment of justice. Every precept the Lord has given is trustworthy because every work He has done reveals truth and justice. This is not abstract doctrine. It means the commandments He gives you are not arbitrary rules imposed by a distant ruler but the very expression of a God whose nature is reliability itself. When you follow His precepts, you are not bending to an alien will; you are aligning yourself with the way reality actually works, ordered by the One who made it.

The psalm culminates in its central truth: the fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom. Not the end—the *beginning*. This is the foundation upon which all true understanding rests. You cannot think clearly about anything that matters without first having encountered the holiness and awesomeness of God's name. Every other kind of wisdom will eventually lead you astray because it is built on a false starting point. But those who begin here, who genuinely tremble before the Lord's majesty while trusting His covenant faithfulness, gain a rich understanding that endures. The psalmist does not promise this understanding will make life comfortable or answer all your questions. He promises it will make you wise—able to see reality as it is, to recognize the fingerprints of a faithful God even in difficult providences, to trust His precepts even when obedience costs you. This is the wisdom that leads to genuine life.

You are reading this psalm in a world that urgently needs people who worship with their minds engaged. The psalmist models something countercultural: someone who stops, examines what God has done, allows the weight of it to settle on him, and then responds with deliberate praise. He does this not alone but in the council of the upright, in the assembly—his joy is meant to be witnessed and shared. Take time today to ponder one work of God. Not to manufacture feeling about it, but to really think about what it tells you about who He is. Then let that thinking become the ground of your praise. This is how you begin to be wise.
